claim.hypotheses_location: Section 1. Introduction: paragraph beginning “Based on previous findings by Matthias et al. (2010), we hypothesized that presentation of an auditory alerting cue…”, which states that alerting increases visual processing speed and that alerting intensity should correlate with processing speed.
claim.statement_location: Abstract: summary stating that phasic auditory alertness increases visual processing speed, lowers the threshold of conscious perception (Exp. 1), that processing speed increases with cue intensity (Exp. 2, replicated in Exp. 3), and that increases in processing speed are accompanied by increased pupil size (Exp. 3).

data.source: Laboratory behavioral experiments (single-letter recognition task with backward masking) and pupillometry (desktop-mounted eye tracker).
data.sample_size: Experiment 1: 28 participants. Experiment 2: 28 participants (after excluding 1 ADHD participant). Experiment 3: 25 participants for behavior (24 for pupillometry due to technical issues).
data.unit_of_analysis: individual participant (TVA parameters estimated per subject; trial-level accuracy and pupil traces recorded)
data.notes: Trials used non-aging (constant hazard) foreperiod distributions for ITI and CTOA. Exposure durations: 10–80 ms. Alerting cues at 0, 40, or 85 dB (500 or 900 Hz, counterbalanced). TVA parameters (v, t0) estimated with a high-threshold guessing component (pg).

method.description: Three accuracy-based experiments assessed how phasic auditory alerting affects early visual perception using a single-letter recognition task with backward masking. In Exp. 1, a high-intensity (85 dB) auditory cue preceded targets on one-third of trials; TVA-based modeling estimated processing speed (v) and perceptual threshold (t0) separately for cue/no-cue conditions. Exp. 2 manipulated alerting intensity (no cue, 40 dB, 85 dB) to test monotonic effects on TVA parameters. Exp. 3 replicated Exp. 2 and recorded pupil diameter as a physiological marker of alertness, using equiluminant stimuli and a retention interval to capture phasic dilations.
steps: Recruit participants with normal or corrected-to-normal vision and normal hearing; screen hearing (Exp. 1).
Present single letters (from a 20-letter set) at 3.4° left/right of fixation for exposure durations 10–80 ms, followed by pattern masks (500 ms).
Manipulate auditory alerting: Exp. 1 (no cue vs 85 dB); Exp. 2/3 (no cue, 40 dB, 85 dB). Cue frequency 500 or 900 Hz to avoid habituation.
Use non-aging distributions: ITI geometric (200 ms steps, hazard 1/3); CTOA geometric (30 ms steps, hazard 2/5).
Instruct participants to report letter identity only when fairly certain; otherwise skip (to limit guessing). Provide feedback after each trial.
Record accuracy across exposure durations per condition; in Exp. 3, record pupil diameter time-locked to cue onset and preprocess (blink interpolation, artifact rejection).
Estimate TVA parameters v (letters/s) and t0 (ms) per subject and condition via maximum likelihood (Nelder–Mead). Include a high-threshold guessing parameter pg.

Statistical analysis: paired-sample t-tests comparing conditions for v and t0; likelihood ratio tests comparing multi-parameter models across conditions; in Exp. 3, compare early and late peak pupil dilations across conditions and correlate pupil peaks with v at specific exposure durations.
method.models: TVA-based single-item encoding model with parameters v (processing speed), t0 (perceptual threshold), and pg (guess rate); maximum-likelihood estimation using Nelder–Mead simplex. Paired-sample t-tests for condition comparisons; likelihood ratio tests for nested model comparisons across conditions; Pearson correlations between pupil dilation peaks and v.
method.outcome_variable: Primary: visual processing speed v (letters/s) and perceptual threshold t0 (ms). Secondary (Exp. 3): peak pupil dilation (mm) in early (300–800 ms) and late (800–2000 ms) windows after cue onset.
method.independent_variables: Alerting condition (no cue vs cue); alerting intensity (40 dB vs 85 dB).
method.control_variables: CTOA and ITI with constant hazard (non-aging) distributions; cue pitch randomized (500/900 Hz); letter side balanced; fixed mask duration; equiluminant colors in Exp. 3; instruction to avoid guessing with pg modeled; normal vision/hearing inclusion; exposure durations counterbalanced across conditions.
method.tools_software: E-Prime 2 for stimulus presentation; Matlab (Nelder–Mead) for parameter estimation; Eyelink 1000 eye tracker for pupillometry; Oscilla USB-310 audiometer for hearing screening; 21" CRT monitor; custom interpolation algorithm for blink/artifact handling.

results.summary: Across three experiments, phasic auditory alerting increased visual processing speed and lowered the perceptual threshold. Exp. 1 showed higher v and lower t0 with an 85 dB cue versus no cue. Exp. 2 demonstrated that v increases monotonically with cue intensity (85 dB > 40 dB > no cue), while t0 is reduced by alerting but does not differ between 40 dB and 85 dB. Exp. 3 replicated these behavioral effects and showed that pupil dilation increased with alerting intensity; early and late peak dilations were larger for 40 dB versus no cue and for 85 dB versus 40 dB. Peak pupil dilation correlated positively with processing speed v at near-threshold exposure durations.

metadata.original_paper_title: The effect of phasic auditory alerting on visual perception
